title: TRL Library
lesson:
TRL stands for Transformer Reinforcement Learning. It is an open-source Python library from Hugging Face whose one job is to train language models after they already know how to talk — the polishing steps that turn a raw model into a helpful one. If Hugging Face gives you the models and PEFT gives you the cheap way to tweak them, TRL gives you the recipes for the actual training run.

What problem it solves

Fine-tuning a model by hand means writing a training loop: feed in data, measure how wrong the model is, nudge its parameters (the millions of internal numbers it learns), repeat. Doing that correctly for preference data or reinforcement learning is genuinely fiddly — easy to get subtly wrong. TRL packages the proven loops into ready-made trainer objects so you bring the data and the model, and it handles the maths.

Analogy 1 — the bread-machine. You could bake bread from scratch: weigh flour, knead, watch the oven, hope. A bread-machine bakes the same loaf reliably — you just choose a program (white, wholemeal, dough-only) and add ingredients. TRL is a bread-machine for fine-tuning. Each trainer is a program; your dataset and base model are the ingredients.

The main programs (trainers)

TRL ships several trainers, each matching a training method you may already know:

  • SFTTrainer — Supervised Fine-Tuning. Show the model good example answers and it copies their style. This is instruction tuning on SFT datasets.
  • DPOTrainer — runs DPO, teaching taste from chosen vs rejected answer pairs.
  • GRPOTrainer / PPOTrainer — full RLHF-style reinforcement learning, where the model tries things and gets a score (a reward) for how good each try was. GRPO is the newer, lighter recipe that powers many 2025–2026 reasoning models.
  • RewardTrainer — trains a small scorer model that rates answers, the piece classic RLHF needs.

Analogy 2 — driving school. SFTTrainer is watching an instructor and copying them. DPOTrainer is the instructor saying "that turn was better than the last one." The RL trainers are letting you drive solo while a points system rewards smooth, safe driving — you learn from your own attempts, not just by copying. The three sit in a natural order: copy first, then learn taste from comparisons, then refine from your own scored attempts.

You normally use these in sequence, not in competition. A typical pipeline is SFTTrainer to teach the basic shape of good answers, then DPOTrainer (or an RL trainer) to refine the taste on top. Trying to skip straight to the harder methods on a model that can't yet follow instructions is like teaching defensive driving to someone who hasn't learned the pedals.

Why it is everywhere

TRL plugs straight into the rest of the Hugging Face stack. It speaks the same Dataset format, loads any model from the Hub, and — crucially — works hand-in-hand with PEFT so you can train a tiny LoRA add-on instead of the whole model. Tools like Unsloth and Axolotl are largely friendly wrappers around TRL: they make it faster or easier to configure, but the trainer underneath is often TRL itself. Learn TRL and you understand what those tools are really doing.

What it looks like in code

A minimal SFT run is genuinely short. You load a model, point a trainer at a dataset, and call train():

from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig
from datasets import load_dataset

data = load_dataset("my/chat-dataset", split="train")
config = SFTConfig(
    output_dir="./out",
    num_train_epochs=1,
    learning_rate=2e-4,   # small: gently refine, don't rebuild
)
trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model="Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B",  # any model from the Hub
    args=config,
    train_dataset=data,
)
trainer.train()

To make this train cheaply on a small GPU, you hand TRL a PEFT config and it only updates the LoRA adapter:

from peft import LoraConfig
trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model="Qwen/Qwen3-0.6B",
    args=config,
    train_dataset=data,
    peft_config=LoraConfig(r=16),  # train a tiny add-on, not all weights
)

Where this matters in real products

Say you run a legal-document assistant. The base model is fluent but too chatty and occasionally bluffs. You collect a few thousand example answers in your house style and run SFTTrainer to teach the format; then you gather pairs where lawyers picked the better of two drafts and run DPOTrainer to lock in tone and caution. That two-step polish — both lines of TRL — is the standard path from "a model" to "your model." The same pattern shows up in coding copilots, support bots, and the reasoning models that learn step-by-step thinking from rewards: different trainer, same library, same plug-into-the-Hub workflow.

💡 TRL is the trainer, not the deployment. Once training finishes you save the adapter or merged checkpoint and serve it with something like vLLM or Ollama. TRL's job ends when training does.
mentalModel: TRL is a bread-machine for fine-tuning: pick a program (SFTTrainer to copy good answers, DPOTrainer to learn taste from pairs, GRPO/PPO to learn from rewards), add your dataset and base model as ingredients, press start. It hides the fiddly training loop so you just choose the recipe.
mistakes: Reaching for an RL trainer when SFT is all you need. GRPOTrainer and PPOTrainer are powerful but slow and finicky. Most tasks are solved by SFTTrainer plus a touch of DPO. Start simple; only escalate if simple fails.
Forgetting peft_config and training the full model. Without a PEFT/LoRA config, TRL updates every weight, which needs far more VRAM and often runs out of memory on a single consumer GPU.

Wrong dataset shape for the trainer. SFTTrainer wants text or chat messages; DPOTrainer wants prompt/chosen/rejected. Feeding DPO-shaped data to an SFT trainer (or vice-versa) fails or trains on nonsense — check the trainer's expected columns first.

Skipping the chat template. Chat models expect a specific format (special tokens marking user vs assistant turns). If your data isn't wrapped in the model's chat template, the model learns the wrong structure and behaves oddly at inference.

Using a learning rate built for pretraining. Fine-tuning is a gentle nudge. A rate like 1e-3 can blow up an already-good model; sane fine-tuning rates are tiny (roughly 1e-4 to 5e-6 depending on the trainer).
Assuming TRL adds new facts. Like LoRA, these trainers change behaviour and style, not knowledge. For fresh, lookup-able facts use RAG, not a TRL run.
exercise:
Install TRL (pip install trl peft) and write a tiny SFT script. Use a small open model (under 1B parameters, so it fits on modest hardware), load any small instruction dataset from the Hub, attach a LoraConfig(r=16), and run SFTTrainer for just a handful of steps. Generate one answer before training and one after, and note any change in style.

Stretch goal: Swap SFTTrainer for DPOTrainer using a small preference dataset (with chosen/rejected columns). What did you have to change about the dataset's shape, and why couldn't you reuse the SFT data as-is?
solution: Hint: A few steps on a tiny model won't transform it — you're checking the plumbing, not chasing quality. For the stretch goal: SFT data is single good answers (one target per prompt), but DPO needs a comparison — two answers per prompt, one labelled chosen and one rejected. You can't reuse SFT data directly because it has no 'rejected' alternative to push the model away from; DPO learns from the gap between the two, so the second answer is essential. Keep the learning rate tiny (around 5e-6 for DPO) and pass peft_config to both so you only train the LoRA adapter.
summary: TRL (Transformer Reinforcement Learning) is Hugging Face's library of ready-made trainers for post-pretraining work: SFTTrainer (copy good answers), DPOTrainer (learn from chosen-vs-rejected pairs), and GRPO/PPO trainers (learn from rewards). It plugs into the Hub and PEFT/LoRA so you train a tiny adapter cheaply, and it underpins tools like Unsloth and Axolotl. Bring data and a model; TRL runs the loop.

keyTerms.def: Transformer Reinforcement Learning: Hugging Face's open-source library of pre-built trainers for fine-tuning and aligning language models.
A ready-made object in TRL that runs a complete training loop for one method (e.g. SFTTrainer, DPOTrainer). You give it a model and data; it handles the maths.
TRL's trainer for Supervised Fine-Tuning: the model copies the style of good example answers. This is how instruction tuning is done in practice.
TRL's trainer for Direct Preference Optimization: it learns taste from preference pairs (a chosen answer and a rejected one) without a separate reward model.
TRL's reinforcement-learning trainers, where the model attempts answers and is improved by a reward score. GRPO is the lighter, newer recipe behind many recent reasoning models.
A small scorer model (trained with TRL's RewardTrainer) that rates how good an answer is, used to guide RLHF-style training.
An optional setting you pass a TRL trainer so it trains only a small LoRA adapter instead of every weight — far cheaper on memory.
The model-specific format (special tokens marking user and assistant turns) your data must be wrapped in so a chat model trains and behaves correctly.
